summary: I was inducted into the Service in Michigan on August 19th, 1943. That was where I first gathered with the group of people I was going to serve with. It was a significant moment because I was just eighteen and fresh out of school, ready to start my military journey.

I attended Crane Tech High School before I was drafted into the Service. I lived nearby on Thomas Street in the North Austin area. It was just before I was inducted in 1943, and I was planning to go to college before the draft changed my plans.

I lived in the North Austin area on Thomas Street when I was going to Crane Tech High School. That neighborhood was where I grew up before entering the Service in 1943. It was a typical community with many young men like me getting ready to be drafted or involved in war projects.

I was drafted into the United States Army Air Forces in 1943 and went through six weeks of basic training at Jefferson Barracks in St. Louis. Although I wanted to be a pilot, I was told I lacked the coordination needed, but they kept me in the Air Force. I was sent to various bases like Del Mar, Texas, and Gulfport, Mississippi, and flew missions all over the world during World War II.

Jefferson Barracks in St. Louis was where I underwent six weeks of basic training after being drafted in 1943. It was the start of my time in the Army Air Forces and where I hoped to become a pilot. Although I didn't make it as a pilot, Jefferson Barracks was a key place in my early military experience.

St. Louis was important to me because I trained at Jefferson Barracks there in 1943. It was my first stop after being drafted into the Army Air Forces. The city marked the beginning of my military career before I was sent to other bases across the country and overseas.

After basic training, I was sent to Del Mar, Texas, as part of my service in the Army Air Forces. It was one of the bases where I was stationed before picking up my B-17 plane. Del Mar was a stepping stone in my journey before heading overseas for combat missions.

Gulfport, Mississippi was where I picked up a brand new Boeing B-17 Flying Fortress. From there, we flew to various locations including Maine and Iceland. Gulfport was a critical place where I transitioned from training to active flying missions during World War II.

I flew a brand new Boeing B-17 Flying Fortress starting from Gulfport, Mississippi. This plane carried me through many missions across the world, including places like Maine, Iceland, the Aleutian Islands, and Europe. It was a tough but reliable aircraft that I flew during my 51 missions in World War II.

Maine was one of the first stops after we picked up our B-17 in Gulfport. From there, we flew to Iceland and then on to other distant locations. Maine was part of the route that took me closer to the European and North Atlantic theaters during the war.

Iceland became like a home base for me during my missions in World War II. We lived in tents near Foggia, close to Naples, but Iceland was a key location where we stayed for a time. It was an interesting and strategic place during the war, and I flew many missions from there.

The Aleutian Islands were among the many places I flew missions to during the war. After leaving the U.S., our route took us through Maine, Iceland, and then the Aleutians. It was part of the vast and challenging areas where I served as a crew member on the B-17.

Madagascar was one of the far-flung locations where I flew missions during World War II. Our flights took us across multiple continents, and Madagascar was part of that extensive route. It was an unusual and memorable place in my military service journey.

Tunisia was another key location where I flew missions during the war. It was part of the North African campaign, and I remember flying there as part of my 51 missions. The conditions were tough, and every day was uncertain, but Tunisia was a significant theater in my service.

We lived in tents near Foggia, close to Naples, around August 1944. It was a harsh and unpredictable environment where we never knew what the next day would bring. Foggia was a base area from which I flew many of my combat missions in Europe.

Naples was near where we were stationed in tents during the summer of 1944. From that area, including Foggia, we launched many missions into Europe. Naples was part of the Italian theater where I served after being deployed overseas.

On November 16th, 1944, I was shot down over Yugoslavia after our plane was hit by flak. Tito's partisans surrounded us and were very friendly, helping us after the crash. I stayed in a town there for a couple of days before making my way alone to Bari, Italy. It was a frightening but memorable experience during my service.

Poland was one of the countries I flew missions over during World War II. It was part of the dangerous European airspace where we faced flak and fighter attacks. Flying over Poland was just one of many challenging parts of my 51 missions.

Germany was a primary target during many of my bombing missions. We flew over German territory multiple times, facing heavy flak and enemy fighters. It was a perilous part of the war, and many of my fellow crew members were lost in these missions.

After being shot down in Yugoslavia, I made my way alone to Bari, Italy. When I arrived, there was still fighting going on, so I hid in the hills until someone picked me up and took me back to base. Bari was a critical place for me to recover and regroup after that harrowing experience.

After flying missions in Italy, I went on to fly missions in France as well. Following my recovery from being shot down, I was assigned to fly a few more missions there. Eventually, I was asked to participate in bond rallies, but I decided I didn’t want to fly anymore.

When I was shot down over Yugoslavia, Tito's partisans surrounded our plane and were very friendly to us. They helped us after the crash, and their support was crucial in that dangerous moment. It was remarkable to be assisted by Tito's forces during the war.

The Tuskegee Airmen played a vital role in protecting us during one of our missions when we were heavily attacked. They were black pilots who provided crucial air cover, and without their help, we might not have made it back to base. Their bravery and skill left a lasting impression on me during the war.
